speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is November 1, 1995. We're with Lucie Porges, maiden name Eisenstab. The interviewer is Brian Cohen. We're in New York City. And we will do it in English. My name is Brian Cohen. Today's date is November 1, 1995. We're doing an interview with Lucie Porges, maiden name Eisenstab. We're in New York City. And we'll be doing the interview in English. Let's begin by having you tell is your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My name is Lucie Porges. It used to be Eisenstab before I was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell Porges?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's P-O-R-G-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Eisenstab.

speaker: interviewee
text: E-I-S-E-N-S-T-A-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Hebrew name is Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is the 23rd of November, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: My current age is 68.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city of the birth is Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's begin by telling, telling us about your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents-- I had one set of grandparents, my father's parents, in Vienna, who were quite Orthodox. My grandmother wore a sheitel. And my grandfather had a beard. And they were very good people, not very worldly. Well, I know my grandfather went to the synagogue every day. And on Friday, he used to bring people home for dinner, always. We didn't see them too much. We saw them maybe every two weeks or so. It was a whole trip. You took a street car. And they lived in a different part of the city than we did. These were my father's parents. And they came from Poland, from Galicia, from a little town called Drohobych, which apparently was next to Lemberg, which was the bigger one. Then I had a grandmother, my mother's mother. I-- my grandfather died during the War of 1914. He had, I think, just a very bad cold. And there was no penicillin. And he just died. I mean, he had a very high fever and died. While they were leaving-- were going into exile-- they were somewhere in Czechoslovakia before reaching Vienna, and he died there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I only knew my grandmother on my mother's side. Now, she lived not too far away from us in the same part of Vienna, which means that I saw her much more often, practically every day. And she was more a part of my life. And I loved her very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a more worldly woman. And I adored her, because she used to tell me lots of stories. And I just loved stories. And my mother just saw her every day. We went to the park when we were children. And she came along. And it was just incredibly close.

speaker: interviewer
text: The, the grandparents on your father's side, what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name was actually Eisenstab-Weichsel, W-E-I-C-H-S-E-L, because of all kinds of documents-- Polish papers, Austrian papers. It was complicated. There were Jewish weddings. And they were not civil weddings.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Vienna, they wound up with two names, which is difficult to understand. But it's not an isolated case. So actually, my father's brother, his only brother, his name was Weichsel. My father's name was Eisenstab. And that's how I knew it. Did he have a profession in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandfather.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather-- I don't really remember what he did. I mean, I was a child. I didn't question it. And I never asked my parents. He wa-- he was doing something, but nothing very important. He was just a very bourgeois, medium kind of man, not a rich man or anything like that-- very simple people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on your mother's side, what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my mother's side, the name was Rosner. And they came from a town which was really a resort. It was called Dornei Vatra. And it was in the, in the Bukovina-- what was called Bukovina-- which was part of the Austrian-Hungarian monarchy. There were all those countries joined together under the emperor Franz Joseph. And people even talked German in all those places. They talked a little bit of Romanian, but they talked a lot of German. They were brought up that way. And I remember-- I, I know more about my mother because she actually did a little tape where she talks about her childhood. They lived in a house in that very nice town. And she had-- they were seven, two girls and five sons. And know that her father dealt in wood. She even told us that he used to ship wood on the rivers. And that was his profession. And I know that they had a little dog. And they had-- it seemed to be like more country-- a countrified life when she was young. She was about 16 or 17 when the war started. And that's when they started emigrating. They went first to Czechoslovakia and then end up in Vienna-- ended up in Vienna. And some of her brothers were in the army. The whole family kind of dispersed. And then they all settled in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were there names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names? My mother's name was Jetty, Jetty Rosner. And my father's name was [? Iztik ?] Eisenstab, which I think would come from Isaac. It was just spelled in a different way in Austria. And he also came that way from Poland with his parents during the war. He had two brothers. One was alive, and one just disappeared in the war somewhere in Russia. They never heard of him again. I know my grandmother was waiting all her life for this son to come back, because it was an unended story. And my other uncles in Vienna started out having all kinds of stores. They had candy stores. They had grocery stores. And my mother worked with them in those stores and then met my father. And I think they got married in 1926-- beginning of 1926-- in a Viennese synagogue that I still-- whenever I go to Vienna, I go and look at it. It's still there. As a matter of fact, it is now the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It is the place where there are lots of documents. When I went there two years ago, I found my birth certificate. And my husband did. And they still have a lot of documentation of things that happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you define what it is now in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: What it is--

speaker: interviewer
text: The institution.

speaker: interviewee
text: The institution-- it's still a beautiful, beautiful building. But it's kind of heavily guarded. Everything is heavily guarded. People are still afraid of, of-- how do you call that-- of things happening. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. How do you call that, the Arabs that attack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sabo-- sabotage? Sabotage-- it's heavily guarded. As a matter of fact, it's-- the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is guarded by Israelis. And you can't just get in. You have to tell who you are and document it. And the synagogue is mostly closed except for special services. Does it hold only Jewish archives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it holds only Jewish archives.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the, the history of Vienna, or from a larger area?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say Vienna and Austria-- and this is where I found out-- this year actually-- I went there. And I asked if they knew anything about my grandparents. And that's a whole other story. I found out a lot of things about my grandparents. And my husband found out about his cousins, what happened to them. They told us everything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father dealt in textiles. He sold textiles. He sold them in Vienna. He worked for a big firm. And he also traveled through Austria with shirtings and wools, English flannels, and things like that. He knew a lot about textiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a sense, when your parents were growing up in Vienna, their reac-- their interaction with the non-Jewish community? And do you think there was, there was anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm sure there was. And I don't think-- well, they came to Vienna when they were teen-- in their teens, you know? They were 18, 19, around that age. And I don't think they had a lot of Viennese friends. I don't think they had a lot to do with Viennese people, actually. That's the sense I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why do you think that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because the were not really ever accepted totally in Vienna. They were just refugees, kind o, immigrants that were not well seen, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Jewish community in general not well-received, or only the immigrants who came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, there were lots of wonderful Viennese Jews that were part of the literati and all that-- writers, playwrights. And they were really the tissue of Vienna. They were part of Vienna-- all these people in 1920, 1930. The most ex-- extraordinary people, artists-- were all Jewish. And of course, they were very known. But they themselves were not very happy about the other Jews that came later. And I don't think there was too much communication. But I was always fascinated by those Jewish writers and the whole life-- the literary life-- in Vienna. I-- even when I-- here and in France where I lived, and Switzerland, I always-- first of all, I spoke German. And I kept it, because I kept talking to my parents, even though learning French and English. And I always read a lot of books about Aus-- about Vienna and all this development. And now when-- whenever I go back, it, it's just fascinating, because I knew about it. But I, I knew about it just in my head. But I never saw all these places where all this happened. And I absolutely fascinated by it. When-- whenever I go back, I now look at everything and study it. And it, it's like a big hole in my life that is being filled again-- things that I wanted to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's speak about your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: My childhood-- I went-- I lived in the 10th District, which was not right in the center of Vienna. And I went to a school where there were not very many Jewish kids-- my sister, too. And then it was rather uneventful, I must say. And my-- when Hitler came, we had to leave the school. And Jewish people just made schools in, in different districts, where we could continue. And it a long time to get there. It was very complicated to get there, from one district to the other, by street car. And it never really worked again. I mean, we went just a little bit. But so much was going on-- raffles, raffles and all that-- that we missed like a whole year of regular schooling then. That was in 1938. Because Hitler came in March, 1938. And all this-- that whole period between March, 1938, and when we left in January, 1939, it's just a few months. But they were full of panic and terrible events. I mean, the whole life was turned upside down for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were-- when you were in public school, did you have many non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. I had more Jewish friends than non-Jewish friends, but not exactly from my class, just that lived closer to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a conscious decision on your part or your parents' part?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it just so happened. No, I guess my parents didn't have a lot of relations with non-Jewish people, and that's why. At that time, I think, you saw mostly your family. On, on Sundays, on holidays, you met your families. You met your grandparents, your cousins, on Jewish holidays. You didn't intermingle a lot with non-Jewish people, which our life is really-- now we do. My husband and I, we have lots of friends of different nat-- nationalities, colors. It's to-- it's much more open. It's a totally different world. It's much more open and broader now. So you just had to understand, it was quite limited.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it was an, and option for you to develop those non-Jewish relationships, or were there-- were they not interested?

speaker: interviewee
text: Possibly I would have developed it, I think, by my living in Vienna, and getting older, and by my interests. I probably would've met many more people. It's just that it wasn't the circle of my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: In childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: In childhood, would you have been able to develop relations with non-Jews if you had made the effort?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose I could have, yes. My husband did. He had a little friend who was a non-Jew. And he's an absolutely marvelous person. When we go to Vienna now, he's still a very good friend of his. So it-- he just lived in a different part of Vienna where there were more non-Jews, and where he had more, more of a relationship with these people. I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: The neighborhood you grew up in, it was in a Jewish sec-- Jewish section?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no it wasn't. It wasn't. But I don't know, my parents were so careful. They were very-- they, they just, they were hovering over us, my sister and I. And we didn't have a chance to meet a lot of other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what it was like in your home on, on a, on a daily, a daily basis.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a daily basis in my home? Well, my father was away a lot. He was traveling. And my mother was a very good Jewish mother. She always, number one, wanted us to eat. And this was an awful thing for me, because I just didn't like to eat very much. Every day, she asked me what I wanted for lunch. And I was just so bored by it, and had like-- in protest. She made me little sandwiches to go to school with. At the end of the week, my school bag was full of half-eaten sandwiches. Because I just didn't feel like eating. It was something I wasn't interested in. And this stayed with me all my life. Because I never bothered my children with eating. They could eat if they wanted to. I gave them nice things. But it wasn't important. And believe it or not, they grew up-- their favorite thing is to go to restaurants, my children. I just don't care about food. I mean, I know what good food is like. And I appreciate it when I have it. But I never really cared about food. Whenever I had money or something, I bought a book. I was into books and things like that-- going to a movie, going to museum-- not food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish holidays were kept in my family. It was wonderful. We usually, for, for the Seder, we went to my grandmother. And I remember my father presiding and sitting in one of those very big chairs with lots of pillows. And it was lovely. We were lots of people. And I remember my mother making special wine out of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], you know, raisins, for the children. Because we just got the raisin win. And the grownups got wine. And I thought it was the most delicious thing. When I grew up and I tasted wine, I thought it was awful. I always expected it to taste like that. And I still don't like wine that much. I like, I like that. That's why I drink a lot of apple juice. It's sweet. It's nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember that. But I also remember that it used to take very, very long. The Seder took forever. It was long to sit there and to wait for it to end and to sing the last song. But it was nice. The family was close. Would it always be in Vienna, or would you sometimes meet at other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was always in Vienna, yeah. And it was mostly on my-- with the family of my mother. Because it was a bigger family-- not with my father's grandparents, with my mother's mother. They were kind of more social.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's, let's talk about clubs that you were involved with, the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when Hitler came, somehow, everybody tried to fit into something. And I went to a thing called [? Blavyse ?], Blue White, which was a Zionist organization. I, I went there just a few times, because it felt good to be with other Jewish children at that point. And it was nice to talk about the country like [? Palestina ?], where there was hope. I mean, it was so horrible in Vienna. It was nice to get together and to have a feeling that something else existed. But I was really never a person that went to a lot of organizations. I, I-- that was just a few times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were from the same kind of background I was. As a matter of fact, in Vienna, as-- for two or three years, I went to school which cal-- which was a private school. It was called [? Bibleschule ?], where I learned to speak Hebrew. It was part of my curriculum. My parents wanted me to know Hebrew. And at this point, I learned Hebrew. And I was quite a good pupil. I remember getting a prize or something. But I must say that I totally forgot it with all these things that happened afterwards. With going to Belgium, and learning French very fast, and all these things, all the events, I, I kind of forgot it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of activities would [? Blavyse ?] be involved with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was games also. It was just games. And there was a lot of talk about [? Palestina ?] and [? Ashara ?] and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many, how many children were involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: There must have been about 20 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: The decision to go public school from Jewish school, was that one that, that your parents made?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was made by the Nazis in Vienna. Because people-- at one point, had to-- they didn't want people to have apartments all over the place. For instance, we had to move to our grandmother's apartment. We had to leave our apartment. And so did a cousin of mine. We all moved into one apartment. And then we had to leave. After Kristallnacht, we have to leave. They took lots of things away from my grandmother's apartment. And we had to go to another place to live with more people together. It was like they were trying to make some kind of a ghetto of Jewish people. And that's when we were supposed to go to a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sense-- did you sense that things were changing in, in the mid '30s, the, the mood, the atmosphere?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not. My parents probably did. I did not realize it, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in school, your, your relations with the non-Jews were, were pretty steady?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew that I was an outsider. I knew, because most people, most kids were Catholics. And they had-- there was an hour where they had Catholic introduction and a special teaching. And there were like two or three children that were Jewish. And they had a Jewish religious hour. And we were just really with, with a, with a rabbi. And we were looked upon as totally different. It was not a pleasant thing. Because children always want to be part of the majority. They don't want to be different. And I realized it then already that we were different. It wasn't pleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so you-- the Jewish weren't involved with the Catholic hour? It happened at the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all, no. They were not part of it. We had something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an option? Could you have been part of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I just was Jewish. I couldn't have been part of it, no. Vienna, that was a very strong Catholic city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, did you get a sense from your parents that, that they understood that things were changing, things we're becoming more ominous?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was such a feeling. But I couldn't really name it exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever speak with you about the situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it clear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it, it was actually, it-- I know it happened, because two of my uncles left Vienna and lived in Germany. They had a very good business in Germany that-- in the '30s, in the early '30s. So things must have been difficult in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father-- did you have a sense that your father was suffering financially? Were Jewish businesses coming under, under attack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember that. I mean after Hitler, yes-- before, I don't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was your first memory of, of things really becoming a problem, was it Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was when Hitler came into Vienna-- I mean, like from one day to another. It was the most awful felling, that you were just free to be heard, free to-- people could do anything. They scared you. We were scared out of our wits from one day to another. It was very ominous. It was horrible. We had a feeling that we were doomed the minute he came to Vienna, because there were threats all the time. They immediately started. Some people were arrested immediately, I would say. Some people that were more known, you know, were arrested immediately or left the country immediately. For us, we were, we were just part of the Jewish community of Vienna, not being especially known to anybody, just like all the masses.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just a horrible feeling of doom. Suddenly, we realized that we probably couldn't stay there, that we have to leave, but we didn't know where. Where did you go from one day to another? Imagine it happening to you in America. You have a home. You have family. And then suddenly, you have to leave. Where do you go, to South America, To Africa? I mean, where do you go? It, it's just horrible. You had to just start thinking in these lines. It was just terrible. Do you remember specific events that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember people getting together and talking and say, what can we do? What's going to happen? It was just very, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people who were creating this sense of doom, were, were they Viennese? Or were they outsiders who came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the horrible thing that they were Viennese, that lots of people worked in the underground. You had no idea. And suddenly, people that were your neighbors, et cetera, came out in a uniform. And they could tell you what to do and not to do and threaten you. That was the horrible feeling from one day to another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was [? Blavyse ?] still functioning during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, yes. I think it mostly functioned during that time, because people tried to get together and talk about it and make sense of it. And usually, there's always-- people get together in times like that. They, they bind together, you know? And it's important.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you remembered the, the conversations of, of the Jews trying to decide what, what they were to do. Were you a part of that conversation? Or were you in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew that my uncles from Germany-- two sons of this grandmother of mine, my mother's mother-- had left already. They had left Germany-- so we knew, because Hitler came there 1933-- and went to Israel. They decided to leave Vienna and go to Israel, because they had a feeling that things weren't going well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first thing that happened, they wanted my grandmother to leave Vienna and go to Israel. And that was an absolutely terrible thing for my mother, to think that her mother was going to leave, that they are going to separate. That was the beginning of everything going badly, of disrupting, you know? So that was a terrible threat. Do you know what the, what the plans of, of your family were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes-- slowly, we, we knew that we should leave Vienna. So my parents, like all the other people, tried to find out how to do it, tried to find out which country would take us, which country would give us a visa. We tried for America. We tried for England. We tried for all kinds of places. But we didn't have family. And we weren't able to get a visa. So at some point after-- my mo-- my grandmother left for Israel. She, she did get the papers. And she left for Israel at some point. I don't remember which month. And on Kristallnacht, which was in November-- there were all these months between March and November where we moved, and moved, and moved to different places-- they arrested my father, put him into prison-- the Gestapo. And then it really became very serious. So my mother ran every single day to a different part of Vienna to get papers that would clear us, that we don't owe Austria money, that we don't, we don't have anything wrong with us, that, that we were never in prison, so that we would get the possibility to leave Austria if we get a passport, and visa for any, anywhere. Because you had to clear everything also from Austria if you wanted to leave. But we didn't get any visa. So my mother also ran all over the place to liberate my father, which she finally succeeded with a lawyer. After six weeks, he came out of prison. And he was told that unless he leaves in the next two weeks, they would send him to Poland. At this point, my mother said, leave. Go, go anywhere. Go to Belgium. Go over the border and leave us, because the children and I, nothing will happen. And my father just didn't want to do that.
